1.1: biblos geneseos (biblos geneseos) — book of the origin/genealogy — the same phrase as LXX Gen 5:1 (haute he biblos geneseos anthropon). Matthew deliberately opens with the creation-formula, presenting Jesus as inaugurating a new genesis. Iesou Christou — Jesus Christ — the name first, immediately followed by both titles: huiou Dauid (son of David, the royal Messianic line) and huiou Abraam (son of Abraham, the covenant promise-bearer). The reverse order (David before Abraham) emphasizes the Messianic dimension; then the founding covenant is named.
1.18: mnesteutheises (mnesteuo) — betrothed, engaged — the Jewish betrothal (erusin) was a legally binding covenant, not merely a modern engagement; breaking it required a divorce (v.19 apolyein = to release/divorce). ek pneumatos hagiou — of/from the Holy Spirit — the preposition ek denotes origin: the conception is sourced from the Holy Spirit, not from human generation. ephanerothe — was revealed, appeared — Matthew uses a passive suggesting divine disclosure; the pregnancy was not hidden but theologically explained by the angel as divine action.
1.21: Iesous — Jesus, the Greek form of Hebrew Yeshua (Joshua), which means YHWH saves or YHWH is salvation. The angel's explanation (autos gar sosei = for he himself will save) plays on the name's etymology: the one named YHWH-saves will in fact save (sosei). ton laon autou — his people — the possessive is striking; even before the birth, the angel calls Israel his people. apo ton hamartion auton — from their sins — not merely from enemies or oppressors (the expected Davidic deliverance) but from sins; the scope of salvation is transformed.
1.23: parthenos (parthenos) — virgin — Matthew uses the LXX reading of Isa 7:14, which translates Hebrew almah (young woman of marriageable age) with the Greek term specifically denoting virginity. Matthew stakes the fulfillment on this reading. Emmanuel — Hebrew Immanu El = God with us — not merely a descriptive title but a theological claim: the presence of God (the Shekinah) is now embodied in the person of Jesus. Matthew frames the entire Gospel between Immanuel (1:23) and the presence-promise of 28:20.

2.1: magoi (magi, plural of magos) — originally a priestly caste of Persian Zoroastrians skilled in astronomy, interpretation of signs, and dream interpretation. By the 1st century the term had broadened to include wise men, court astrologers, and interpreters of omens throughout the eastern Mediterranean. Matthew does not specify three or kings — the church tradition of three kings derives from the three gifts (v.11). apo anatolon — from the east — the same direction from which the Babylonian exile came; now wise men from the nations seek the Jewish King.
2.11: prosekynesan auto — they worshiped/did obeisance to him — proskyneo (fall before in worship or homage) is used throughout Matthew for worship of Jesus (4:10, 8:2, 9:18, 14:33, 15:25, 28:9,17) and specifically for worship of God (4:10 quoting Deut 6:13). The Magi perform before the infant what the disciples perform before the risen Lord. edoran auto dora — they offered him gifts — the noun dora (gifts, offerings) carries cultic overtones; these are tribute-offerings appropriate to a king and priest.

3.2: metanoeite (metanoeo) — repent, change one's mind and direction — the verb combines meta (after, change) + noeo (to think, perceive). True repentance is not mere remorse (metamelomai) but a reorientation of the whole person's direction. hengiken gar he basileia ton ouranon — for the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near — engiken is perfect tense (has come near and stands near), indicating the kingdom's arrival is not merely future but has approached to proximity. Basileia ton ouranon is Matthew's distinctive phrase (vs. Mark/Luke's basileia tou theou); the ouranon (heavens plural) is the reverential Jewish circumlocution for God.
3.11: baptizo (baptizo) — to immerse, dip, plunge — used for dyeing cloth, sinking ships, and ritual washings; in Jewish contexts (mikveh) it denoted purification immersion. John's baptism is eis metanoian (unto/for repentance) — the preposition eis indicates purpose or result. He contrasts it with Jesus's baptism en pneumati hagio kai pyri — in/with the Holy Spirit and fire — the preposition en is instrumental: the Spirit and fire are the medium, not the destination.
3.17: houtos estin ho huios mou ho agapetos — this is my beloved Son — the demonstrative houtos identifies the specific individual; agapetos (beloved, uniquely loved) appears in the LXX at Gen 22:2 for Isaac (the only son, yahid); the term carries the Aqedah resonance of the uniquely beloved son offered. en ho eudokesa — in whom I am well pleased — the aorist eudokesa (to be well pleased, to take delight in) combines the royal Ps 2:7 investiture with the Servant Isa 42:1 anointing; king and servant in one declaration.

4.1: anechthe (anago) — led up — the passive (was led up) makes the Spirit the agent of the wilderness-leading, just as YHWH led Israel in the wilderness (Deut 8:2, the immediate context of Jesus's first temptation response). peirasthēnai — to be tested/tempted — peirazo carries both meanings: external testing (to prove quality) and internal temptation (to induce sin). The wilderness testing recapitulates Israel's 40 years (Jesus's 40 days) with the key difference that Jesus passes where Israel failed.
4.17: apo tote erxato — from that time he began — apo tote is a Matthean structural marker used at key transitions (also 16:21 for the passion-announcements). kerusso — to herald, proclaim — the verb of public announcement rather than private teaching; Jesus speaks as one with royal authority proclaiming the King's news. The message is identical to John's (3:2): metanoeite, engiken gar he basileia ton ouranon — but Jesus proclaims it as the one in whom the kingdom is present, not merely approaching.

5.3: makarioi (makarios) — blessed, happy, fortunate — in Hellenistic usage the word described the blissful state of the gods or those who lived like gods; in Jewish usage (Hebrew ashre) it described the one who is in a right relationship with YHWH and experiences his favor. The plural (blesseds) is a Hebraism. hoi ptochoi to pneumati — the poor in spirit — ptochos is the beggar-poor (from ptosso, to crouch) rather than the merely hard-pressed penes; in spirit (dative) specifies the domain: those who know their total spiritual bankruptcy before God.
5.17: katalyo — to abolish, dissolve, tear down — used for dismantling buildings and nullifying covenants; Jesus uses the strongest possible abolition-verb to deny that his intention is abolition. pleroō — to fill full, complete, fulfill — the same verb as the formula citations (hina plerothe). Jesus comes not to add to the law but to bring it to its intended fullness and goal; his life, death, and resurrection are what the Torah was pointing toward. iota hen e mia keraia — one iota or one tittle — the smallest letter of the Greek alphabet (iota = yod in Hebrew) and a serif-mark on a letter; not the smallest detail will pass unfulfilled.
5.21: ercousin is the formula ekouste hoti errethe — you have heard that it was said — the passive errethe (it was said) leaves the speaker unnamed, placing the authority on the received tradition. ego de lego hymin — but I say to you — the ego is emphatic by position; the antithesis is not Moses vs. Jesus but tradition-understood vs. authoritatively-interpreted. Jesus speaks with exousia (authority from the one who has the right to speak), not like the scribes who derived authority by citation.
5.48: esesthe oun hymeis teleioi — you therefore shall be perfect/complete/mature — teleios (from telos, end/goal) means having reached one's full end or purpose; it is not the impossible Platonic ideal of moral perfection but the wholeness/undividedness of character that mirrors God's wholeness. The parallel in Luke 6:36 reads oiktirmon (merciful), suggesting teleios here means wholeness-in-love, the same indiscriminate love that God shows to all. hos ho pater hymon ho ouranios teleios estin — as your heavenly Father is perfect — the standard is not an abstract ideal but the character of the Father.

6.9: Pater hemon ho en tois ouranois — Our Father who is in the heavens — the address combines intimacy (Pater = Father, probably translating Aramaic Abba) with transcendence (en tois ouranois = in the heavens, indicating divine majesty). hagiastheto to onoma sou — hallowed be your name — the passive imperative (hagiastheto) is a divine passive: may God himself act to hallow his name (sanctify it before the world). This is not a prayer that we will reverence the name but that God will act eschatologically to vindicate his own name (Ezek 36:23).
6.12: aphes hemin ta opheilemata hemon — forgive us our debts — opheilema (debt) is the commercial-legal term for what is owed; sin as moral debt to God is a Semitic conceptual framework (Aramaic hoba = debt = sin). The aorist aphes (forgive, once) vs. the perfect in aphekamen (we have forgiven, already done) — the community's forgiveness of others is presented as the completed precondition, not a parallel request.
6.24: mammonas (mammonas) — Mammon — an Aramaic loanword (mammon), wealth or possessions as a personified power; Jesus personifies wealth as a rival lord (kyrios). ou dynasthe theo douleuein kai mammona — you cannot serve God and Mammon — the impossibility is structural: a slave belongs entirely to one master; divided service is definitionally impossible in the slave-master relationship. The two masters are presented as mutually exclusive rivals for ultimate loyalty.

7.24: oikodomesen — built — the aorist indicates completed action, the wise builder actually builds (vs. merely hearing). akouon mou tous logous toutous kai poion autous — hearing my words and doing them — the two-stage requirement mirrors the Shema (shema = hear and obey). petra — bedrock, solid rock — contrasted with psammos (sand); the Semitic image of the rock as foundation for covenant stability (Isa 28:16, the tested cornerstone in Zion). The house built on rock withstands floods and winds — the twin disasters of Palestinian flash floods and seasonal windstorms.

8.9: kai gar ego anthropos eimi hypo exousian — for I also am a man under authority — the centurion's argument is a fortiori: if he can command soldiers at a distance because he himself is under Roman military authority (a chain of command that gives his words power), how much more can Jesus command disease at a distance, being himself under the ultimate authority. exousia — authority, the right and power to act — appears 10 times in Matthew, always associated with Jesus's teaching, healing, and forgiving (7:29, 9:6, 11:27, 28:18).
8.17: autos tas astheneias hemon elaben kai tas nosous ebastasen — he himself took our infirmities and bore our diseases — Matthew's citation of Isa 53:4 applies the Servant's vicarious bearing not to the cross but to the healing ministry. Labo (took) and bastazo (bore, carried) are the verbs of the Servant's substitutionary work. The healing miracles are not merely humanitarian acts but are themselves the Servant's carrying of human sickness — an extension of the same substitutionary principle that reaches its fullness in the passion.

9.6: exousian echei ho huios tou anthropou epi tes ges aphienai hamartias — the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — the two claims in sequence: (1) the Son of Man (Danielic title) exercises divine prerogatives, (2) epi tes ges (on earth) — this authority is exercised in history, not deferred to final judgment. The scribes' objection is theologically correct (only God can forgive sins); Jesus's claim is equally precise: the Son of Man is acting with God's authority on earth.
9.13: eleos thelo kai ou thysian — mercy I desire and not sacrifice — the Hosea citation (Hos 6:6); eleos (steadfast covenant-love, translating Hebrew hesed) is the Covenant-quality Jesus consistently prioritizes over external cultic performance. ouk elthon kalesai dikaious alla hamartoulous — I did not come to call righteous people but sinners — kaleo (to call) carries the election-language of divine summoning; the call of sinners is the unexpected reversal that defines Jesus's mission-profile.

10.28: me phobeisthe apo ton apokteinonton to soma — do not fear those who kill the body — the first me phobos is addressed to the fear of persecutors; the second phobeisthe de mallon (rather fear) addresses the appropriate fear of God. The contrast is not between two fears of the same kind but between the fear that produces obedience to God and the paralysis produced by fear of humans. ton de psychen me dynamenon apokteinai — those who cannot kill the soul — soul (psyche) as the seat of personal existence that physical death does not terminate.

11.28: deute pros me pantes hoi kopionte kai pephortimenoi — come to me all who labor and are heavy laden — kopionto (laboring to exhaustion) + pephortimenoi (perfect passive, permanently burdened) describes the condition; deute pros me (come to me) is Wisdom's invitation. anapausis — rest, refreshment — not idleness but the rest of the Sabbath: cessation from the burden of self-justifying religious effort. chrestos (v.30) — good, pleasant, kind — the same word used for good wine that has aged well (Luke 5:39) and for the kindness of God (Rom 2:4); the yoke of Christ has the quality of what has been made right.

12.31: blasphemia tou pneumatos — blasphemy against the Spirit — uniquely among Matthew's sin-categories, this sin is ouk aphethesetai (will not be forgiven) in this age or the coming age. The context defines the blasphemy: attributing the works of the Holy Spirit (exorcism performed by the Spirit of God, v.28) to Beelzeboul (the devil). The sin is the persistent, seeing refusal to recognize the Spirit's presence in Christ's ministry — a willful inversion of light and darkness that places oneself permanently outside the reach of the Spirit who grants repentance.
12.35: ho agathos anthropos ek tou agathou thesaurou ekballei agatha — the good man out of his good treasure brings forth good things — thesauros (treasury, storehouse) as the inner reservoir from which speech flows; ekballo (to throw out, bring out) emphasizes that speech is not accidental but driven out of what is stored within. The heart-treasury precedes and determines the word: character produces speech, not vice versa.

13.11: hoti hymin dedotai gnona ta mysteria tes basileias ton ouranon — to you it has been given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of the heavens — mysterion (mystery, secret) in Jewish apocalyptic usage denoted the divine plan hidden in the age but now disclosed to the elect; Daniel uses the term for what only God and his messenger can reveal (Dan 2:47). The disciples receive the gift of understanding (synesis) that the parable-audience does not receive — not by their merit but by divine grace (dedotai, divine passive).
13.52: grammateus matheteutheis te basileia ton ouranon — a scribe discipled to the kingdom of the heavens — the concluding self-description of the kingdom-teacher; Matthew presents Jesus as producing a new kind of scribe who draws from both old and new. kainos kai palaios — new and old — the order is deliberate (new first): the new covenant does not discard the old treasury but brings it forward into its fulfillment; the scribe who understands the kingdom reads the old Torah through the new light Christ provides.

14.27: tharsein — take courage, be of good cheer — an imperative of encouragement used in crisis moments; Jesus speaks it to the storm-panicked disciples and to the paralytic (9:2) and to the hemorrhaging woman (9:22). ego eimi — I am — the LXX divine self-disclosure formula (Exod 3:14, Isa 41:4, 43:10); spoken in the darkness on the sea to terrified disciples, the formula identifies Jesus with the divine presence that crossed the Red Sea. me phobeisthe — do not fear — the standard divine reassurance formula throughout the OT (Gen 15:1, Isa 41:10, 43:1).

15.8: ho laos houtos tois cheilesin me tima, he de kardia auton porro apechei ap emou — this people honors me with the lips but their heart is far from me — Matthew cites Isa 29:13 LXX as the proof-text for the Pharisees' tradition-over-Torah priority. Kardia (heart) in the Jewish anthropology is the seat of will, understanding, and intention — not merely emotion; far-heartedness is not coldness of feeling but absence of genuine covenantal alignment. matēn de sebontai me — in vain do they worship me — maten (vainly, to no purpose) makes the worship not merely imperfect but empty, producing nothing that constitutes true worship.

16.16: su ei ho Christos ho huios tou theou tou zontos — you are the Christ the Son of the living God — Peter's confession stacks three titles: Christos (the anointed one = the Messiah), huios tou theou (Son of God = the one in a unique filial relationship to the Father), and tou zontos (of the living God = the God who is distinctively, characteristically alive, contrasted with dead idols). The full Christological confession combines royal Messianism, divine sonship, and the living covenant-God.
16.18: su ei Petros kai epi taute te petra oikodomeso mou ten ekklesian — you are Peter (Petros) and on this rock (petra) I will build my church — the wordplay is clearer in Aramaic where both are kepha (Cephas). The referent of petra is debated: Peter himself as the founding confessor, Peter's confession of Christ, or Christ himself as the foundational rock (1 Cor 10:4). Matthew's context suggests Peter-as-confessor: the one who receives the revelation (v.17) is the one on whom the community is built. ekklesia — assembly, congregation — Matthew is the only Gospel to use the word; it translates Hebrew qahal (the covenant assembly of Israel).

17.5: nephele photeinen — a bright/luminous cloud — the cloud of divine presence (shekinah) throughout the OT (Exod 13:21, 40:34-38, 1 Kgs 8:10-11); the Transfiguration cloud is the visible form of the divine glory-presence investing itself in the scene. akouete autou — listen to him — the addition to the baptism-declaration (3:17) echoes Deut 18:15 (him you shall listen to = the prophet like Moses); the Transfiguration completes the Moses/Elijah pattern by declaring that the one who surpasses them is the one to be heard.

18.18: hosa ean desete epi tes ges estai dedomena en ourano — whatever you bind on earth will have been bound in heaven — the exact same binding/loosing language as 16:19 (the authority given to Peter) is now given to the community as a whole in the context of church discipline. Deo/luo (bind/loose) were standard rabbinic terms for declaring something forbidden or permitted (halakic ruling); here applied to the community's disciplinary decisions and intercessions. The perfects (dedemenon/lelymeron) indicate that the heavenly ratification precedes and grounds the earthly decision.

19.4: ho ktisas ap arches arsen kai thely epoiesen autous — the one who created from the beginning made them male and female — Jesus grounds the marriage argument in the arche (beginning = creation order), which has theological priority over Mosaic legislation. The creation-given (ktisas, aorist passive) is the theological bedrock from which human institutional arrangements derive; Moses's concession accommodated fallen humanity but did not abrogate the creation norm. The two-become-one-flesh (Gen 2:24) is not a Mosaic law but a creational institution that precedes law.

20.28: dounai ten psychen autou lytron anti pollon — to give his soul as a ransom in exchange for many — lytron (ransom price) is the price paid to release a slave or captive; the LXX uses it for the redemption-payment. Anti (in exchange for, instead of) is a substitutionary preposition: the soul of Jesus stands in the place of the many. Psyche here means life/person-as-a-whole; Jesus gives himself entirely as the exchange-price. The Isaianic Servant background (Isa 53:10-12) provides the theological content: the Servant makes his soul an offering for sin and thereby justifies many.

21.42: lithon hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomountes houtos egeneto eis kephalen gonias — the stone which the builders rejected, this one became the head of the corner — apedokimasan (rejected after examination, found to be unfit) is the technical term for the stone-mason's rejection of a stone after inspection. Kephalen gonias (head of the corner) denotes either the capstone (keystone completing an arch) or the cornerstone (the principal foundation stone that sets the line of the building). Both readings apply Christologically: the rejected Jesus becomes the load-bearing stone of the new community. The citation is Ps 118:22-23 LXX.

22.37: agapeseis kyrion ton theon sou en hole te kardia sou kai en hole te psyche sou kai en hole te dianoia sou — you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and all your soul and all your mind — the Shema (Deut 6:5 LXX) adds dianoia (mind, understanding) to the MT's heart-soul-strength, creating a triad that covers the affective, volitional, and cognitive dimensions of the whole person. The totality-markers (holos = whole, in all three cases) indicate that the love commanded is total — leaving no part of the person divided or uncommitted.
22.44: eipen kyrios to kyrio mou — the LORD said to my Lord — the LXX renders Ps 110:1's YHWH ladon (YHWH to my Adonai); Jesus's argument turns on David calling his own descendant Lord (kyrios), which would be unseemly if the son were merely human. The Davidic messianic expectation assumed the son-of-David would be David's inferior; Jesus uses Ps 110:1 to demonstrate that the Messiah is simultaneously son and superior — a paradox that points to the divine sonship.

23.23: apodekatoun to heduosmon kai to anethon kai to kyminon kai aphekate ta baryteta tou nomou — you tithe mint and dill and cumin and neglect the weightier matters of the law — baryteta (weighty things, the heavy end of a scale) is the metaphor: some laws have greater weight than others. The three weighty matters named are krisis (justice/judgment), eleos (steadfast covenant-love/mercy), and pistis (faithfulness); these are the OT prophetic priorities (Mic 6:8, Hos 6:6) that the scribal tradition had flattened by treating every regulation equally.

24.30: kai tote phanesetai to semeion tou huiou tou anthropou en ourano — and then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven — the sign (semeion) is disputed: whether the cross appearing in the sky, or the Son of Man himself as the sign, or the Shekinah-cloud. The citation of Dan 7:13 that follows (erchomenon epi ton nephelos tou ouranou) suggests the sign is the appearing of the Son of Man himself in the divine glory-cloud. kopsontai pasai hai phylai tes ges — all the tribes of the earth will mourn — citing Zech 12:10 (the mourning that follows seeing the one they pierced); grief and recognition converge at the parousia.

25.40: eph hoson epoiesate heni touton ton adelphon mou ton elachiston emoi epoiesate — inasmuch as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me — the identification of Jesus with the least (elachistos, the lowest-ranked, the most inconsequential) is the theological core of the passage. The phrasing eph hoson (to the degree that, inasmuch as) makes the identification proportional and complete: every act of mercy to the suffering is simultaneously an act toward Jesus himself. This is not metaphor but an ontological claim about Christ's solidarity with human suffering.

26.28: touto gar estin to haima mou tes diathēkēs to peri pollon ekchynnomenon eis aphesin hamartion — for this is my blood of the covenant which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins — the phrase draws together four OT streams: (1) covenant-blood of Sinai (haima tes diathēkēs, Exod 24:8); (2) Servant for many (peri pollon, Isa 53:12); (3) Jeremianic new covenant (Jer 31:31-34); (4) Zechariah's blood-of-the-covenant (bedam beritecha, Zech 9:11). The cup-word is a compressed Christological commentary on the whole OT sacrificial and covenantal tradition.

27.46: eli eli lema sabachthani — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — the Aramaic form (Matthew uses transliterated Aramaic, Mark has Aramaic too; both differ slightly from each other and from the LXX) reproduces the opening of Ps 22:1. The question hina ti (why, for what purpose) is not a denial of God's sovereignty but a genuine cry from within the experience of abandonment. Jesus prays the whole Psalm, which ends in vindication (Ps 22:24-31); the cry of dereliction is the beginning of the Passion Psalm, not its whole message. The darkness and the cry together communicate the full weight of bearing divine wrath on behalf of others.

28.18: edothe moi pasa exousia en ourano kai epi ges — to me has been given all authority in heaven and on earth — edothe (has been given, divine passive) grounds the authority in the Father's gift, not in Christ's inherent right alone (though the divine sonship implies it); the crucified one now speaks as the enthroned one. The scope is total (pasa exousia = all authority) and universal (en ourano kai epi ges = in heaven and on earth) — the Danielic authority of the Son of Man (Dan 7:14) now historically enacted in the post-resurrection commission.
28.20: idou ego meth hymon eimi pasas tas hemeras heos tes synteleias tou aionos — behold I am with you all the days until the completion of the age — idou (behold, a call to attention) introduces the climactic promise. Ego eimi (I am) is the divine-presence formula; pasas tas hemeras (all the days = every single day, not merely always in general) stresses the daily-accompanying reality. The frame of the Gospel (Immanuel = God with us, 1:23; I am with you always, 28:20) is the Matthean inclusio: the Gospel's subject is the presence of God incarnate, permanently dwelling with his people to the end of the age.
